metadata.title: Why Cancer Is Evolution Running Inside the Body
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Biology candidate 17. Mechanism: Darwinian clonal selection assembles colorectal cancer one driver mutation at a time over decades — independent tumors converge on the same mutations in the same order because selection keeps finding the same growth advantages.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = the winning / selected clone that expands; CRIMSON = the mutation / selection event; GOLD = editor's-pen highlight (fill only, once per graphic); SLATE = structural scaffolding; INK = all text. Never swap mid-film.

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on cream newsprint; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; accumulate move — each mutation stage widens the TEAL fan
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, ink type, TEAL for the winning clone, CRIMSON for each driver mutation event

metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO driver vs passenger mutation statistics detail, NO Lynch syndrome / MSI, NO chromothripsis, NO mutation rate or mutational signature discussion, NO synthetic lethality concepts. Two accents only: TEAL (winning clone) and CRIMSON (mutation event). Manim move: accumulate — clone fan widens at each stage transition as the new driver appears.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Colorectal cancer carries four specific driver mutations, in a specific order — patient after patient. That pattern requires an explanation.

card.copy: Why Cancer Is Evolution Running Inside the Body

beats.narration_text: Bert Vogelstein's group biopsied colorectal tissue at four successive stages — normal, small adenoma, large adenoma, carcinoma — and sequenced each. APC appeared first. Then KRAS. Then 18q loss. Then TP53.

props.headline: Bert Vogelstein's group biopsied colorectal tissue at four successive stages —…

beats.scene_description: a printed research-paper figure of the adenoma-carcinoma sequence, a left-to-right flowchart of four tissue stages with mutation labels on arrows, pinned to aged newsprint, flat editorial reproduction
beats.image_prompt: printed page from a molecular biology research paper showing the Vogelstein adenoma-carcinoma sequence — a horizontal four-stage flowchart: normal mucosa, small adenoma, large adenoma, carcinoma — with driver mutation labels on the arrows between stages, printed in black ink on white paper, pinned to aged cream newsprint, desaturated editorial collage reproduction

beats.narration_text: Each mutation appeared at a distinct stage transition. Each provided a growth advantage. Cancer was not an event — it was assembled incrementally over years.

production_viz.label: four stages, one mutation each — the timeline
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal timeline: four stage labels (NORMAL, SMALL ADENOMA, LARGE ADENOMA, CARCINOMA) connected by arrows; a CRIMSON mutation chip drops onto each arrow in sequence — APC, KRAS, 18q, TP53
production_viz.note: first appearance of the accumulate move — CRIMSON event chips land one at a time

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] stage timeline with CRIMSON mutation chips landing

beats.narration_text: If mutations were random, each tumor would carry a different gene set. Instead, independent tumors converge on the same four mutations in the same order. Randomness cannot produce convergence at this scale. Something is selecting.

card.copy: Why do independent cancers converge on the same mutations in the same order?
card.sub: if mutation is random, why does the path repeat?

beats.narration_text: The default model: mutation is random copying error. Thousands of genes can be hit. The probability of the same four genes, in the same order, across independent tumors is vanishingly small under random expectation.

production_viz.label: random mutation expectation — scattered dots
production_viz.mechanic: a grid of many grey dots (genes); for each of three 'tumors', a small random scatter of CRIMSON dots highlights different genes — no pattern, no overlap
production_viz.note: establishes the naive expectation before the mechanism beats reverse it

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] random scatter — three tumors hit different genes

beats.narration_text: Vogelstein found a consistent sequence — not occasionally the same, but always. Three independent tumors: identical order. Randomness is ruled out. The path is constrained.

production_viz.label: same path, three independent tumors
production_viz.mechanic: three rows, each a small horizontal sequence of mutation chips; all three rows carry the identical order: APC, KRAS, 18q, TP53 — in crimson — aligned in columns to show the convergence
production_viz.note: visual contrast with B05 — same path every time vs random scatter

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three tumor rows — identical driver sequence aligned

beats.narration_text: Cancer is Darwinian evolution operating inside the body. Three conditions apply: heritable variation — mutations arise. Differential reproduction — some cells divide faster. Selection — faster dividers displace slower ones.

One normal crypt cell acquires an APC mutation. Wnt growth signaling increases. Its daughters crowd out neighbors. Over years the APC-mutant clone fills multiple crypts. The first expansion fan has formed.

production_viz.label: stage 1: APC mutation — the first expansion fan
production_viz.mechanic: a single TEAL cell at the left; as APC chip (CRIMSON) drops, the TEAL fan grows wider — a wedge expanding rightward labeled 'APC clone'; a small CRIMSON chip annotates the mutation event; timeline arrow runs left to right
production_viz.note: the accumulate move begins — fan widens at each stage

beats.narration_text: Within that APC clone, one cell acquires a KRAS mutation. RAS locks into the active state; growth signals fire continuously. That sub-clone grows faster and produces a small polyp visible at colonoscopy.

production_viz.label: stage 2: KRAS mutation — second fan within the first
production_viz.mechanic: the APC fan from B08 persists; inside it a KRAS chip (CRIMSON) drops; a second, even wider TEAL fan emerges from within the first, labeled 'KRAS sub-clone'; the sub-clone fan is brighter / more saturated teal
production_viz.note: accumulate: each stage adds a wider fan nested inside the prior one

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] KRAS sub-clone fan widens inside APC fan — stage 2

beats.narration_text: TP53 loss in one KRAS/APC cell removes the damage checkpoint. Progeny ignore stop signals. A large adenoma forms. Subsequent invasion through the basement membrane marks carcinoma.

production_viz.label: stages 3 and 4: TP53 loss then invasion — fan complete
production_viz.mechanic: continuing fan: TP53 chip drops (CRIMSON), the widest fan yet emerges — labeled 'TP53 sub-clone'; a dashed basement-membrane line below the fan is crossed as the tip shades darker — the carcinoma
production_viz.note: accumulate final stage — the full four-stage fan is now visible on screen; the fans narrow competitors stay thin alongside

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] TP53 fan — carcinoma tip crosses basement membrane

beats.narration_text: Selection explains convergence. APC loss activates Wnt — the strongest growth lever in the colon. Any cell that loses APC outcompetes neighbors, so APC is always first. KRAS provides the next largest advantage. TP53 loss bypasses damage arrest. The order is fixed by the size of the advantage at each stage.

production_viz.label: why the order is fixed — selective logic
production_viz.mechanic: three rows, each a gene chip (APC, KRAS, TP53) in CRIMSON beside a brief serif label of the growth advantage it provides; a TEAL arrow to the right of each row grows longer to indicate the size of the advantage — APC longest, then KRAS, then TP53
production_viz.note: the answer to THE QUESTION — selection forces a consistent order

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] three-row selection logic: gene, advantage, TEAL arrow

beats.narration_text: Cancer risk rises steeply with age because accumulating the right mutations in the right order takes decades. Carcinogens accelerate the mutation rate and compress that timeline — cancer comes earlier.

production_viz.label: decades of selection — age and cancer risk
production_viz.mechanic: a horizontal timeline labeled 0 to 60 years; four CRIMSON chips drop at spaced intervals along the timeline (APC, KRAS, 18q, TP53); below each chip a small TEAL wedge grows, culminating in the carcinoma at year 60; implies time is the selection window
production_viz.note: no mutation rate numbers — just the time window concept

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] age-risk timeline — four chips across decades

beats.narration_text: Illustrative single case. One crypt stem cell. Year zero, APC mutation: daughters fill three adjacent crypts over five years. Year five, KRAS G12D in one APC-mutant cell: small polyp, colonoscopy-visible. Year thirteen, TP53 loss in one KRAS/APC cell: large adenoma. Year seventeen: invasion. One cell, four mutations, seventeen years — assembled, not born.

production_viz.label: illustrative single-patient timeline (numbers labeled illustrative)
production_viz.mechanic: a left-to-right timeline with four labeled milestones: yr 0 (APC, one cell), yr 5 (KRAS, three crypts), yr 13 (TP53, large adenoma), yr 17 (invasion); at each milestone a CRIMSON chip drops and a TEAL expansion fan widens; all numbers labeled with a small MONO 'illustrative' tag
production_viz.note: all numbers are illustrative as required by card content; MONO font for years

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] illustrative patient timeline with four milestones

beats.narration_text: Colorectal cancer converges on the same four mutations in the same order because selection — not chance — governs which mutations persist. Each driver provides the largest available growth advantage at that stage. Independent tumors find the same path because the selective logic is the same in every colon.

card.copy: Cancer finds the same path because selection keeps finding the same advantages.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: This is part of the Cancer Biology series from Humanitarians AI.

props.tagline: AI education for humanitarian and social-impact practitioners.
